target.title: refactor: internalize OpenClaw agent runtime
target.body:
## Summary

This PR removes the old Pi-shaped agent/runtime architecture from OpenClaw and re-integrates that functionality as OpenClaw-owned core, plugin, and SDK surfaces.

The main result is that OpenClaw no longer treats Pi as an embedded dependency or organizing concept. Agent execution, model/provider handling, sessions, compaction, transcript storage, provider startup, plugin runtime loading, and docs/tests now use OpenClaw names, OpenClaw ownership boundaries, and OpenClaw package/runtime contracts.

At a high level this PR:

- Extracts an OpenClaw-owned `@openclaw/agent-core` package from the previously embedded agent runtime code.
- Renames Pi-era source modules, tests, config surfaces, docs, QA scenarios, and type shims into OpenClaw agent/runtime concepts.
- Removes legacy provider/model compatibility behavior that was only there because Pi used to be a dependency boundary.
- Moves provider-owned behavior and dependencies into the owning provider/plugin packages instead of keeping them in root/core.
- Consolidates duplicated session, transcript, compaction, ACPX, Google stream, JSON parser, and test helper logic.
- Keeps compatibility only where it is a real plugin/public boundary, and marks those compatibility paths as deprecated instead of letting them look like internal architecture.
- Updates license/notice text for the incorporated Pi-derived code now that the code is internalized.
- Updates documentation, QA scenarios, test shards, dependency ownership metadata, and package/build wiring to match the new architecture.

## Why

The previous structure still had Pi concepts in places where OpenClaw should own the abstraction directly:

- package names and type declarations still referred to Pi agent/core/coding-agent concepts
- model/provider code still had fallback and alias behavior inherited from the dependency relationship
- sessions and transcript writing had parallel stacks instead of one canonical OpenClaw path
- provider runtime and plugin runtime code carried compatibility branches for old Pi/plugin layouts
- root dependencies included provider-specific packages that should belong to provider plugins
- docs and QA scenarios taught users and maintainers a Pi-era architecture that no longer matched the product

That made the refactor look like a dependency shuffle instead of a real integration. This PR makes the structure look like Pi had never existed as a separate internal dependency: OpenClaw owns the runtime core, providers own provider behavior, plugins cross into core through the SDK/manifest contracts, and compatibility is explicit/deprecated at the public boundary only.

## Architectural Changes

### OpenClaw Agent Core

Adds a new workspace package at `packages/agent-core`.

This package now owns the reusable agent runtime primitives that were previously internalized from the Pi dependency shape:

- agent loop orchestration
- harness execution types
- message conversion helpers
- prompt template helpers
- compaction primitives
- branch summarization utilities
- session interfaces and storage abstractions
- JSONL and memory-backed session repositories
- shell output/truncation helpers
- runtime dependency injection points
- validation helpers
- Node runtime helpers

The package is exported as `@openclaw/agent-core` and wired into root package/build config, workspace config, TypeScript config, and package manager metadata.

Core OpenClaw code now imports the shared runtime contracts from this package instead of reaching through Pi-named local modules or dependency shims.

### Agent Runtime Naming And Ownership

Renames Pi-era agent files and tests under `src/agents` into OpenClaw-owned names.

Examples of the broad rename/integration:

- `pi-auth-*` became `agent-auth-*`
- `pi-bundle-*` became `agent-bundle-*`
- `pi-hooks` became `agent-hooks`
- `pi-settings` became `agent-settings`
- `pi-tools*` became `agent-tools*`
- `pi-project-settings*` became `agent-project-settings*`
- `pi-model-discovery*` became `agent-model-discovery*`
- `pi-agent-core.d.ts` became `agent-core.d.ts`

The goal is not just cosmetic. The renamed modules now sit behind OpenClaw runtime ownership:

- agent runtime ids are OpenClaw runtime ids
- agent settings are OpenClaw agent settings
- bundled MCP materialization is OpenClaw agent bundle materialization
- tool definitions and tool policies are OpenClaw agent tool contracts
- compaction hooks are OpenClaw agent hooks

### Runtime And Plugin Boundary Cleanup

The PR tightens the boundary between core and plugins.

Core remains plugin-agnostic and no longer carries provider/plugin behavior simply because the old dependency needed it. Provider-specific defaults, catalogs, auth choices, discovery, and stream behavior move toward provider-owned packages or focused SDK seams.

Important changes include:

- provider runtime selection no longer routes through legacy Pi runtime names
- old provider alias normalization paths are removed or moved to explicit command/provider-facing compatibility where needed
- provider discovery fallbacks that existed for the old dependency graph are removed
- provider auth bridge behavior that referenced Pi concepts is removed
- provider runtime model types are made more explicit
- runtime plugin loading now uses OpenClaw plugin/runtime contracts instead of Pi package graph assumptions
- startup model ownership moves to manifests/provider catalogs instead of a static root model catalog
- bundled plugin metadata, gateway startup plugin ids, and runtime plugin tests are updated accordingly

### Provider And Model Refactor

This PR removes the old static model-catalog shape from core and replaces it with OpenClaw/provider-owned registry behavior.

Key points:

- static generated model catalogs are removed from runtime ownership paths
- model registration and lookup go through OpenClaw LLM registry code
- provider model ownership is resolved through provider/plugin metadata rather than hard-coded Pi-era catalog assumptions
- GitHub Copilot policy models are sourced from the provider path rather than duplicated core state
- model selection tests are updated for OpenClaw provider ids and current provider ownership
- provider alias handling is narrowed to command/user-facing interpretation where still needed
- legacy provider compatibility paths are removed from hot runtime code

The new LLM area under `src/llm` owns core model/provider contracts and built-in provider implementations:

- API registries
- model registries
- image model registries
- model utilities
- provider stream wrappers
- provider OAuth helpers
- provider diagnostics/event streams
- provider JSON parsing/sanitization utilities
- built-in provider registration

### Provider Dependencies Move To Owning Extensions

Provider-only dependencies are moved out of root/core and into the owning provider/plugin packages.

The most visible case is AWS/Bedrock:

- AWS SDK dependencies move to the Amazon Bedrock extension packages.
- Bedrock provider stream/runtime behavior is owned by the Bedrock extension.
- Root package metadata and lock/shrinkwrap files are updated so root no longer pulls provider-only AWS dependencies.
- Dependency ownership metadata is updated to describe the new package boundary.

Other provider packages and shrinkwraps are updated similarly where dependencies were previously inherited from the Pi/root dependency shape.

### Session And Transcript Unification

This PR consolidates session storage and transcript writing around OpenClaw-owned config/session modules and the new agent-core session abstractions.

Changes include:

- session version ownership moves into config session code
- transcript append helpers are shared instead of duplicated
- transcript headers and JSONL writing are centralized
- session load/store code is updated to use OpenClaw session contracts
- agent session SDK surfaces are updated to point at OpenClaw-owned session types
- gateway/session and command/session tests are updated for the new session model

This is intended to remove the old split between a Pi session stack and an OpenClaw session stack.

### Compaction And Context Runtime Cleanup

Compaction and context-engine code is updated to use the OpenClaw agent-core/runtime shape.

The PR:

- moves shared compaction primitives into `packages/agent-core`
- removes Pi-specific compaction constants/names
- updates compaction docs and QA scenarios to OpenClaw terminology
- updates context-engine host compatibility references
- keeps compaction/session behavior wired through OpenClaw runtime contracts
- adjusts tests to validate the renamed runtime paths

### ACPX Runtime Turn Deduplication

The ACPX plugin had duplicated lazy turn/run-turn adapter logic in both registration and service paths.

This PR extracts that into `extensions/acpx/src/runtime-turn.ts` and updates both callers to use the shared helper.

That shared helper now owns:

- lazy runtime resolution for `startTurn`
- compatibility adaptation from legacy `runTurn` runtimes to `startTurn`
- queued event delivery for legacy run-turn streams
- terminal result conversion from ACP done/error events
- cancellation and stream-close behavior

The behavior stays equivalent, but the implementation is now one path.

### Google And Vertex Stream Projection Deduplication

`src/llm/providers/google.ts` and `src/llm/providers/google-vertex.ts` had near-identical streaming loops.

This PR moves the shared projection into `consumeGoogleGenerateContentStream(...)` in `src/llm/providers/google-shared.ts`.

The shared path handles:

- stream start/done events
- first response id retention
- text block start/delta/end events
- thinking block start/delta/end events
- thought signature retention
- function call/tool call projection
- duplicate or missing tool call id handling
- stop reason mapping
- forcing `toolUse` when tool calls are present
- usage metadata conversion
- cost calculation
- abort/error handling

A new focused test, `src/llm/providers/google-shared.test.ts`, locks the shared projection behavior across text, thinking, tool calls, response ids, stop reasons, and usage/cost accounting.

### Unsafe Integer JSON Parser Deduplication

Ollama had a local copy of the unsafe-integer-preserving JSON parser that already existed in core agent transport code.

This PR promotes that parser to a narrow generic SDK subpath:

- `src/plugin-sdk/json-unsafe-integers.ts`
- `openclaw/plugin-sdk/json-unsafe-integers`

The Ollama extension now imports the shared parser through the SDK boundary instead of duplicating the implementation or reaching into core internals.

Package exports, SDK entrypoint metadata, SDK docs, and plugin SDK export checks are updated for the new subpath.

### Test Fixture Deduplication

`src/agents/sandbox/workspace-skills-bridge-readonly.test.ts` repeated the same remote shell runner fixture in multiple cases.

This PR extracts that fixture into one typed local helper inside the test file. The tests keep the same assertions, but the duplicate shell runner setup is gone.

### Duplicate Scan Cleanup

After the final cleanup pass, duplicate scanning reports zero clones across the configured duplicate scan targets:

- production: 0 clones
- tests: 0 clones
- src-mixed: 0 clones
- extensions-mixed: 0 clones
- test-mixed: 0 clones

This specifically removed the remaining ACPX, Google/Vertex, Ollama JSON parser, and sandbox test fixture duplicate reports.

## Plugin SDK And Compatibility

The plugin SDK is updated to expose only intentional OpenClaw seams.

Changes include:

- new `agent-core` and agent-session SDK forwarding surfaces
- provider/LLM SDK entrypoints for shared provider runtime contracts
- narrow provider stream/model helper updates
- `json-unsafe-integers` as a generic parser utility for plugin-owned runtime code
- SDK baseline/export metadata updates
- documentation updates for SDK subpaths and migration guidance

Compatibility is kept where it is a real public/plugin boundary, not where it was merely internal implementation convenience.

Old public compatibility paths are represented through explicit compatibility/deprecation metadata and tests instead of being treated as current internal architecture.

## Legacy Pi Cleanup

This PR removes Pi as an internal runtime/package concept.

Removed or renamed areas include:

- Pi runtime internals
- Pi package graph tests
- Pi model fixture naming
- Pi agent/core type names
- Pi auth/model discovery names
- Pi docs pages and scenario names
- Pi-specific config/test shard names
- Pi wording in user-facing docs where the subject is now OpenClaw agent runtime

Remaining compatibility is intentionally narrow and boundary-owned:

- public SDK compatibility/deprecation metadata remains where old external plugin imports may still exist
- doctor/migration code retains explicit deprecation handling for real shipped config states
- docs/license notices explain incorporated code provenance

## Documentation Changes

The documentation is updated to describe the new OpenClaw-owned architecture.

Major doc changes include:

- adds `docs/agent-runtime-architecture.md`
- renames `docs/pi-dev.md` to `docs/openclaw-agent-runtime.md`
- removes the old `docs/pi.md`
- updates agent runtime, agent loop, queue steering, model/provider, context engine, compaction, status, migration, and plugin architecture pages
- updates plugin bundle and SDK docs to reflect runtime ownership and plugin boundaries
- updates provider docs where provider behavior moved to provider/plugin ownership
- updates QA scenario names and docs away from Pi/Codex-specific legacy wording where OpenClaw runtime wording is now correct

## Licensing And Notices

Because this PR internalizes code that originated in the Pi dependency shape, it updates repository notices:

- `LICENSE` gets the relevant additional copyright/provenance text.
- `THIRD_PARTY_NOTICES.md` records incorporated Pi-derived code.
- The changelog notes the agent runtime internalization/refactor.

## macOS App Cleanup

The macOS app no longer carries its own static model catalog loader path from the old model-catalog architecture.

Removed:

- `apps/macos/Sources/OpenClaw/ModelCatalogLoader.swift`
- `apps/macos/Tests/OpenClawIPCTests/ModelCatalogLoaderTests.swift`
- associated debug/constants wiring for that removed catalog path

The app should consume the current OpenClaw model/provider surfaces instead of maintaining a parallel static catalog loader.

## Build, Tooling, And CI Metadata

The PR updates build/test/tooling metadata to match the new package/runtime layout:

- workspace package metadata for `packages/agent-core`
- root package exports/dependencies
- package manager lock/shrinkwrap data
- `tsconfig` and `tsdown` package/build wiring
- plugin SDK entrypoint metadata and generated baseline hashes
- dependency ownership metadata
- test project/shard names
- CodeQL workflow/query references for renamed runtime boundaries
- docs/i18n and QA scenario references
- duplicate scan coverage remains valid

## Review Shape

The branch is organized as a stack of focused commits rather than one giant mechanical blob.

Commit themes:

- extract the OpenClaw agent core package
- remove Pi runtime internals
- document the OpenClaw agent runtime cleanup
- decouple and make agent core runtime explicit
- consolidate compaction/runtime/session handling
- move model/provider ownership out of static Pi-era paths
- remove provider Pi auth and legacy runtime selection paths
- trim provider legacy paths and discovery fallbacks
- move CLI runtime ownership to plugins
- drop core provider alias normalization
- dedupe remaining helper implementations

## Behavior Notes

Expected behavior should stay the same for normal OpenClaw users, except that internal ownership, docs, diagnostics, and developer-facing names now reflect OpenClaw instead of Pi.

Intentional behavior/architecture changes:

- provider-only dependencies are no longer root/core dependencies when a provider plugin owns them
- provider/model fallback paths that existed only for the old Pi dependency boundary are removed
- static root model catalog behavior is replaced by provider/manifest/runtime ownership
- internal callers are migrated to current OpenClaw SDK/runtime surfaces
- compatibility is treated as deprecated public boundary support, not internal architecture

## Verification

Behavior addressed: fully internalize the old Pi-shaped agent runtime into OpenClaw-owned core/plugin/SDK surfaces; remove Pi-era runtime/provider/session duplication; keep only explicit deprecated compatibility at public/plugin boundaries; confirm no duplicate scan findings remain.

Real environment tested: local macOS checkout on branch `refactor/openclaw-agent-runtime-cleanup` at pushed head `f650426d547d8d2b15d2cef3ca228dddd51dbdc3`; AWS Crabbox Linux runner for broad changed gate before the final rebase, provider `aws`, lease `cbx_9b165c987860`, run `run_958f0472af21`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sandbox/workspace-skills-bridge-readonly.test.ts src/llm/providers/google-shared.test.ts extensions/acpx/register.runtime.test.ts extensions/acpx/src/service.test.ts extensions/ollama/src/stream.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/api-baseline.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-message.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- `node scripts/check-plugin-sdk-subpath-exports.mjs`
- `node scripts/sync-plugin-sdk-exports.mjs --check`
- `node scripts/check-extension-plugin-sdk-boundary.mjs`
- `git diff --check origin/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm dup:check:json`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --no-web-search`
- Crabbox/AWS broad gate: `pnpm crabbox:run -- --idle-timeout 90m --ttl 240m --timing-json --shell -- "env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_TEST_PROJECTS_PARALLEL=6 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 pnpm check:changed"`

Evidence after fix:

- focused Vitest: 8 files passed, 45 tests passed
- build: passed, including plugin SDK export verification
- plugin SDK subpath export check: passed
- plugin SDK package export sync check: passed
- extension/plugin SDK boundary check: passed
- whitespace diff check: passed
- duplicate scan: 0 clones in production, tests, src-mixed, extensions-mixed, and test-mixed
- autoreview: `autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported`
- Crabbox/AWS `pnpm check:changed`: exit 0 on run `run_958f0472af21`, lease `cbx_9b165c987860`

Observed result after fix: OpenClaw builds with the new `@openclaw/agent-core` package and renamed runtime surfaces; focused ACPX/Ollama/Google/plugin-SDK/sandbox tests pass; SDK exports are consistent; plugin boundary guards pass; duplicate scanner reports zero clones across all configured targets; broad changed gate passed on AWS Crabbox before the final rebase; the branch is rebased onto current `origin/main` and pushed at `f650426d547d8d2b15d2cef3ca228dddd51dbdc3`.

What was not tested: full release packaging, full live provider authentication against every provider, macOS app UI runtime behavior, and a successful post-rebase Crabbox rerun. A post-rebase Crabbox attempt was made, but one AWS lease lost SSH after sync before command start and a retry lease never opened SSH during bootstrap; those were infrastructure failures before tests executed, not test failures. Post-rebase local focused tests and build passed.

Adds a bundled `copilot` plugin with a GitHub Copilot SDK agent harness, docs/QA inventory, on-demand SDK installer, Plugin SDK export changes, labeler updates, and lockfile changes.

PR surface: Source +5210, Tests +8699, Docs +784, Config +63, Other +92. Total +14848 across 55 files.

target.title: [AI] feat(copilot): add GitHub Copilot agent runtime
target.body:
## Round 2 review fixes (2026-05-24)

Addressed all three reviewer findings on the previous head `d81505c539`: P1 ΓÇö bridged tools now honor the resolved sandbox context (caller-side `resolveSandboxContext` in `attempt.ts` plus `sandbox`/`spawnWorkspaceDir` forwarded through `tool-bridge.ts`); P2 ΓÇö `@github/copilot-sdk` is now an optional `peerDependency` with an on-demand install (~260 MB) wired into the wizard via `src/commands/copilot-sdk-install.ts` and a typed dynamic loader in `extensions/copilot/src/sdk-loader.ts`; and the unrelated `plugin: meeting-notes` labeler block is restored in `.github/labeler.yml`. Validated locally with `tsgo:core` + `tsgo:extensions` clean, `check:import-cycles` 0, `extensions/copilot` 19 files / 424 tests passing (was 18/409 before the fixes), 14 new tests in `src/commands/copilot-sdk-install.test.ts`, 4 in `src/agents/copilot-routing.test.ts`, and a live gateway chat-completion smoke against `github-copilot/gpt-4.1` returning the literal expected reply with full prompt-token usage (22,469 tokens including the bootstrap-files context).

## Summary

Adds a bundled `copilot` plugin that registers an opt-in **agent runtime**
backed by [`@github/copilot-sdk`](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@github/copilot-sdk)
and the GitHub Copilot CLI. Selection is fully **config-driven**. The runtime registers via
  `api.registerAgentHarness({ id: "copilot", ... })` and is selected by
  setting `agentRuntime.id: "copilot"` on a model or provider entry.

- What changed:
  - New bundled plugin `extensions/copilot/` (harness + 13 bridges +
    doctor probes + tests).
  - Workspace bootstrap support so the Copilot agent receives
    `IDENTITY.md`, `SOUL.md`, and `HEARTBEAT.md` (parity with the
    bundled PI / Codex runtimes).
  - Docs: new `docs/plugins/copilot.md`; additions to
    `docs/concepts/agent-runtimes.md` and `docs/concepts/models.md` so
    the selection rules are discoverable.
  - QA capability inventory `qa/copilot-capabilities.md` pinning the
    runtime against `@github/copilot-sdk@1.0.0-beta.4`.
  - `.github/labeler.yml` entry for `extensions: copilot`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary):
  - **No source-tree changes outside `extensions/copilot/` and docs/QA.**

## Motivation

OpenClaw users currently can't run any OpenClaw agent using the GitHub
Copilot SDK as the agent runtime. This change adds the copilot agent
runtime as a peer to `pi` / `codex` so the same agent definition can be
flipped between runtimes via one config field, while PI remains the safe
default for everyone else.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature
- [x] Docs

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: route an agent attempt through the GitHub
  Copilot SDK runtime instead of PI, opt-in per model or per provider.
- Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu (Linux), OpenClaw built from this
  branch, gateway running locally against a real GitHub Copilot
  subscription token.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```jsonc
  // ~/.openclaw-copilot-play/openclaw.json
  {
    "agents": {
      "defaults": {
        "model": { "primary": "github-copilot/gpt-4.1" },
        "models": {
          "github-copilot/gpt-4.1": { "agentRuntime": { "id": "copilot" } }
        }
      }
    },
    "plugins": { "entries": { "copilot": { "enabled": true } } },
    "gateway": {
      "mode": "local",
      "http": {
        "endpoints": {
          "chatCompletions": { "enabled": true },
          "responses":       { "enabled": true }
        }
      }
    }
  }
  ```

  ```bash
  corepack pnpm install
  corepack pnpm build
  OPENCLAW_GITHUB_TOKEN=<redacted> \
    node openclaw.mjs --profile copilot-sdk-play gateway run \
      --port 18790 --bind loopback \
      --auth token --token <redacted>

  curl -sS -X POST \
    -H 'Authorization: Bearer <redacted>' \
    -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
    http://127.0.0.1:18790/v1/chat/completions \
    --data '{"model":"openclaw/main","stream":false,
             "messages":[{"role":"user",
               "content":"reply with only POST-UPSTREAM-CLEAN-PORT-OK"}]}'
  ```

- Evidence after fix (gateway log + HTTP response, redacted):

  ```
  [gateway] auto-enabled plugins for this runtime without writing config:
    - github-copilot/gpt-4.1 model configured, enabled automatically.
  [gateway] agent model: github-copilot/gpt-4.1 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
  [gateway] http server listening
    (9 plugins: acpx, browser, canvas, copilot, device-pair,
     file-transfer, memory-core, phone-control, talk-voice; 0.9s)
  [gateway] ready
  ```

  ```json
  {
    "id": "chatcmpl_92b6e47c-e067-46bf-ad7d-45989f6dd288",
    "object": "chat.completion",
    "model": "openclaw/main",
    "choices": [{
      "index": 0,
      "message": { "role": "assistant",
                   "content": "POST-UPSTREAM-CLEAN-PORT-OK" },
      "finish_reason": "stop"
    }],
    "usage": { "prompt_tokens": 22456,
               "completion_tokens": 11,
               "total_tokens": 22467 }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the gateway loads the `copilot` plugin
  alongside the other bundled plugins; the agent attempt is dispatched
  through the `copilot` runtime; the reply comes back through the
  Copilot SDK transport. Removing `agentRuntime.id` falls back to PI
  with no other change.
- What was not tested: BYOK adapters (explicitly out of scope â€” the runtime is
  hard-bound to the canonical `github-copilot` provider).
- Before evidence: with `plugins.entries.copilot.enabled` false (or
  absent), the `agentRuntime.id: "copilot"` selector resolves to
  nothing and the attempt falls back to PI via the existing
  `selectAgentHarness` path â€” no other behaviour change for hosts that
  don't opt in.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A â€” feature add.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A â€” no bug.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New opt-in agent runtime id `copilot`, selectable via
  `agentRuntime.id` on a model entry or `runtime.id` on a provider
  entry.
- New bundled plugin entry `plugins.entries.copilot.enabled`. When the
  entry is disabled or absent, the runtime id is unknown to the
  registry and the resolver falls back to PI with no behaviour change
  for existing users.
- `auto` selection never picks `copilot` â€” selection is always
  explicit.
- `/btw` for a `github-copilot/*`-model agent under the `copilot`
  runtime uses the same in-tree PI fallback path
  (`src/agents/btw.ts` â†’ `streamSimple` via the `github-copilot`
  provider) that any other runtime would. The runtime does not
  implement `runSideQuestion`, so there is no SDK-specific `/btw`
  divergence to maintain.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[agent attempt] -> [PI runtime] -> [Copilot HTTP via the
                                    github-copilot provider]

After (only when agentRuntime.id === "copilot"):
[agent attempt] -> [copilot runtime] -> [@github/copilot-sdk +
                                          GitHub Copilot CLI]

Otherwise unchanged:
[agent attempt] -> [PI runtime] -> [unchanged path]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` â€” the runtime reads tokens the
  same way the rest of OpenClaw does (`GITHUB_TOKEN` /
  `OPENCLAW_GITHUB_TOKEN` / the existing `github-copilot:` auth-profile
  prefix / the logged-in Copilot CLI user). No new env vars are read
  and no new auth-profile namespace is added.
- New/changed network calls? `Yes (additive)` â€” outbound HTTPS to the
  Copilot endpoints the bundled SDK targets (same surface that
  subscription Copilot users already hit via
  `extensions/github-copilot/`). No new third-party hosts.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes (additive)` â€” the
  runtime can invoke the local `copilot` CLI via `@github/copilot-sdk`
  for SDK transport. `probeCopilotCliVersion` in
  `extensions/copilot/src/doctor-probes.ts` documents and gates this in
  `openclaw doctor`.
- Data access scope changed? `No`. Per-agent `copilotHome` keeps
  Copilot CLI state isolated under
  `~/.openclaw/agents/<agentId>/copilot` by default.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: see Risks / Mitigations
  below.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: WSL Ubuntu (Linux); the build itself also succeeds on Windows.
- Runtime/container: Node 22 via Corepack / pnpm.
- Model/provider: `github-copilot/gpt-4.1` from the bundled
  `extensions/github-copilot` provider catalog.
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A â€” gateway running locally
  (`gateway.mode: "local"`, chat-completions HTTP endpoint enabled).
- Relevant config (redacted): see Real behavior proof above.

### Steps

1. `corepack pnpm install && corepack pnpm build`.
2. Point `OPENCLAW_HOME` (or `--profile`) at a profile with the
   snippet above.
3. Run the `gateway run` command and the `curl` POST shown above.

### Expected

- `runtime-plugins` stage loads the bundled `copilot` plugin.
- The runtime's `supports({ provider: "github-copilot",
  requestedRuntime: "copilot" })` returns `{ supported: true,
  priority: 100 }`.
- Attempt dispatched through `runCopilotAttempt`; reply returned by
  the Copilot SDK transport.
- Gateway returns the OpenAI-shaped chat-completion response with the
  literal answer.

### Actual

- Matches expected. Removing `agentRuntime.id` (or disabling the
  plugin entry) reverts to PI for the same model with no other change.

## Evidence

Repeatable from a fresh clone of `openclaw/openclaw` with this branch
checked out on top of upstream `main`:

```bash
corepack pnpm install
# (clean install)

corepack pnpm check:import-cycles
# Import cycle check: 0 runtime value cycle(s).

corepack pnpm tsgo:core
# (no errors)

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions
# (no errors)

node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/copilot
# Test Files  19 passed (19)
#       Tests  424 passed (424)

node scripts/test-projects.mjs \
  src/agents/btw.test.ts \
  src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts \
  src/agents/harness/registry.test.ts \
  src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts
# Test Files  4 passed (4)
#       Tests  108 passed (108)

corepack pnpm build
# build-all completed (dist/extensions/copilot/ produced)
```

Live gateway smoke (redacted token), already shown under
"Real behavior proof":

```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
{"choices":[{"message":{"content":"POST-UPSTREAM-CLEAN-PORT-OK"}, ...}], ...}
```

### Sandbox-enabled live verification (P1 end-to-end proof)

Beyond the unit tests for `resolveSandboxContext` wiring, I stood up
a real Docker sandbox in WSL Ubuntu 24.04 and ran the bundled Copilot
runtime against it. Profile config (`~/.openclaw-copilot-sdk-play/openclaw.json`):

```json
"agents": {
  "defaults": {
    "model": { "primary": "github-copilot/gpt-4.1" },
    "models": {
      "github-copilot/gpt-4.1": { "agentRuntime": { "id": "copilot" } }
    },
    "sandbox": {
      "mode": "all",
      "backend": "docker",
      "workspaceAccess": "rw",
      "docker": { "image": "openclaw-sandbox:bookworm-slim" }
    }
  }
}
```

Built the default sandbox image via `bash scripts/sandbox-setup.sh`,
restarted the gateway, sent a chat completion asking the agent to
write a file. Gateway log shows the sandbox config was loaded and the
tool policy applied:

```
[agents/tool-policy] tool policy removed 3 tool(s) via sandbox tools.deny: cron, gateway, nodes
[agents/tool-policy] tool policy removed 5 tool(s) via sandbox tools.allow: agents_list, message, tts, web_fetch, web_search
```

Docker container created from our config and bind-mounted to the
agent's workspace:

```
$ docker ps --filter ancestor=openclaw-sandbox:bookworm-slim
openclaw-sbx-agent-main-f331f052  Up About a minute  openclaw-sandbox:bookworm-slim  "sleep infinity"

$ docker inspect openclaw-sbx-agent-main-f331f052 --format '{{json .Mounts}}'
[{ "Type":"bind",
   "Source":"/root/.openclaw/workspace-copilot-sdk-play",
   "Destination":"/workspace",
   "Mode":"z", "RW":true, "Propagation":"rprivate" }]
```

The bridged file-write tool (now receiving the resolved sandbox
context after the P1 fix) wrote into the container's `/workspace`
rather than to the host:

```
$ docker exec openclaw-sbx-agent-main-f331f052 cat /workspace/SANDBOX-PROOF.txt
HELLO-FROM-SANDBOX-PR-86155

$ cat /root/.openclaw/workspace-copilot-sdk-play/SANDBOX-PROOF.txt
HELLO-FROM-SANDBOX-PR-86155
```

The host file is present because the workspace bind-mount is `rw`
(matching the configured `workspaceAccess`). Without the P1 fix the
bridged tools would have received `sandbox: undefined` and written
straight to the host workspace, bypassing the container entirely.

### On-demand install transcript (P2 end-to-end proof)

Cleared `~/.openclaw/npm-runtime/copilot/` and invoked the install
helper directly (the same function the wizard hook calls):

```
$ rm -rf /root/.openclaw/npm-runtime/copilot && \
    node_modules/.bin/tsx -e \
    'import { installCopilotSdk, isCopilotSdkInstalled, COPILOT_SDK_FALLBACK_DIR, COPILOT_SDK_SPEC } \
       from "./src/commands/copilot-sdk-install.ts"; \
     console.log(`before: ${isCopilotSdkInstalled()}`); \
     await installCopilotSdk({ logger: console.log }); \
     console.log(`after:  ${isCopilotSdkInstalled()}`);'

before: false
[copilot] installing @github/copilot-sdk@1.0.0-beta.4 into /root/.openclaw/npm-runtime/copilot ...
added 6 packages in 3s
[copilot] installed @github/copilot-sdk@1.0.0-beta.4
after:  true
```

The same install fires from the wizard the first time a user selects
a `github-copilot/*` model and confirms the prompt (see
`src/wizard/select-model-runtime.ts`).

Required-evidence checklist:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after â€” unit + targeted vitest
      pass for `extensions/copilot` (19 files / 424 tests) and the
      core agent surface (4 files / 108 tests). Before this branch, the
      plugin does not exist, so the dedicated tests don't run; the
      core-agent suite is unchanged at the file level vs `origin/main`
      apart from the new `bootstrap-files.test.ts`, which exercises the
      shared bootstrap path the new runtime opts into.
- [x] Trace/log snippets â€” gateway log + chat-completions response
      above.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording â€” N/A (CLI / HTTP).
- [ ] Perf numbers â€” N/A (no perf claim).

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI):

- Verified scenarios:
  - `corepack pnpm check:import-cycles` â†’ `0 cycle(s)`.
  - `corepack pnpm tsgo:core` clean.
  - `corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions` clean.
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/copilot` â†’ 424/424.
  - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/btw.test.ts
    src/agents/harness/selection.test.ts
    src/agents/harness/registry.test.ts
    src/agents/bootstrap-files.test.ts` â†’ 108/108.
  - `corepack pnpm build` produced `dist/extensions/copilot/`.
  - Live dogfood: `github-copilot/gpt-4.1` with
    `agentRuntime.id: "copilot"` returns a real assistant turn through
    the local gateway. Same config with `agentRuntime` removed routes
    back through PI with no other change.
  - Workspace bootstrap dogfood: with `IDENTITY.md`, `SOUL.md`, and
    `HEARTBEAT.md` in the workspace, the Copilot agent receives and
    references their contents (parity with PI / Codex).
- Edge cases checked:
  - Runtime selection: `supports()` rejects any provider other than
    `github-copilot`.
  - Runtime selection: `supports()` returns `{ supported: false }`
    (not `auto`) when `requestedRuntime` is unset, so `auto` never
    lands here by accident.
  - Plugin disabled / absent â†’ the runtime id is unknown to the
    registry, `selectAgentHarness` falls back to PI, no crash.
  - `/btw` on a `copilot`-runtime agent goes through the in-tree PI
    fallback (`btw.ts` â†’ `streamSimple` via the `github-copilot`
    provider), exercising the same path the codebase has used for
    every `github-copilot/*`-model agent on `origin/main` for a long
    time. The runtime deliberately does not implement
    `runSideQuestion`; a regression that re-introduces it lights up
    in `extensions/copilot/harness.test.ts`.
- What I did **not** verify:
  - Any non-`github-copilot` provider routing through this runtime â€”
    explicitly out of scope.
  - TUI surfaces â€” the runtime addition is not related to TUI;

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` â€” pure additive. No existing config field
  changes meaning. Without `agentRuntime.id: "copilot"` (or with the
  plugin entry disabled / absent), the new runtime is dormant.
- Config/env changes? `No new env`. One new optional plugin entry
  `plugins.entries.copilot` with a tiny `configSchema` (only
  `pool.idleTtlMs`).
- Migration needed? `No`.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: bundling vs third-party policy â€” `CONTRIBUTING` notes that most
  features should ship as third-party plugins.
  - Mitigation: this mirrors the precedent set by `extensions/codex/`
    (bundled, first-party agent runtime). `publishToNpm: true` is set
    on `openclaw.plugin.json`, so flipping it out-of-tree is a
    single-flag decision for maintainers.

## Note for maintainers

This is an **AI-assisted** PR. `CHANGELOG.md` is intentionally left
untouched per `AGENTS.md` / ClawSweeper. Suggested changelog entry on
land:

> Agent runtimes: add the bundled `copilot` plugin as a peer agent
> runtime backed by `@github/copilot-sdk` and the GitHub Copilot CLI,
> selectable per-model or per-provider via
> `agentRuntime.id: "copilot"`; the canonical `github-copilot`
> provider is claimed by the runtime's doctor contract. PI remains the
> default and `auto` never selects `copilot`.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 4 constrained provider capabilities
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR keeps WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and BlueBubbles honest by making hosted, device-bound, self-hosted, Mac-hosted, and bridge-backed constraints visible instead of pretending they are equivalent official bot APIs. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 3 official channel capability matrix
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR prevents the long tail from becoming a new pile of native branches. It expresses official app/API platforms as broker aliases and capability rows first, and it now enforces declared delivery capabilities before outbound handoff. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 3 - Official/app platform capability matrix - Role: Adds official/app platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. - Head: `f1a62e9f8a` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `f1a62e9f8a` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 3 official/app channel capability matrix for Channel Broker. - Keeps the work at the broker contract/catalog layer: no native channel migration defaults change in this PR. - Stacks on Phase 2D Slack proof (#86157), Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86113 Related #86105 Related #86108 Supersedes closed PR #86144, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Adds canonical broker platform aliases while keeping `platform: string` open for provider-owned long-tail targets: - `teams` / `msteams` -> `microsoft-teams` - `googlechat` -> `google-chat` - `qq` -> `qqbot` - Aligns `channels.channel-broker.*.capabilities` config with the SDK capability shape: - `delivery` - `live` - `receive` - `native` - Adds focused proof for Microsoft Teams, Google Chat, Matrix, LINE, Feishu, QQ bot, Zalo, Mattermost, Nextcloud Talk, Twitch, IRC, Nostr, Tlon, and Synology Chat. - Documents the Phase 3 platform matrix and SDK capability shape. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Official/app channel aliases normalize through the broker catalog, and Phase 3 platforms route through provider-owned broker requests without enabling native migration defaults. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:teams:19:meeting-channel"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "teams-alias", "messageId": "native-teams-alias", "platform": "microsoft-teams", "conversation": { "id": "19:meeting-channel", "type": "channel" }, "requirements": { "text": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path canonicalized the Teams alias to `microsoft-teams` and produced a provider-owned broker request with no platform-specific native extension branch. - **What was not tested:** Live Teams, Google Chat, Matrix, regional, or self-hosted provider traffic; this PR proves the broker catalog/schema/conformance layer and leaves broad validation to GitHub Actions. ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/platforms.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - `node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` - `pnpm dlx --config.resolution-mode=highest markdownlint-cli2 --config config/markdownlint-cli2.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/plugins/sdk-channel-broker.md` Note: `pnpm exec markdownlint-cli2 ...` was unavailable in the local workspace, so the documented `pnpm dlx` path was used. It linted the docs glob cleanly. ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - This PR does not move native routing defaults for any Phase 3 channel.

sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2D - Slack opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. - Head: `36b82cb22c` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Normalize broker-prefixed Slack channel thread targets such as `broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "channel"` and `threadId`. - Canonicalize Slack broker session routes to the resolved platform target, preserving direct-route semantics as `slack:direct%3A` and thread routes as `slack:?threadId=`. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Slack DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and Slack `threadTs` as broker `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "slack-thread", "messageId": "native-slack-thread", "platform": "slack", "conversation": { "id": "C12345678", "type": "channel", "threadId": "1716500000.000001" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced the expected Slack provider request and kept threaded delivery represented by the broker `threadId` field. - **What was not tested:** Live Slack API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - No native Slack migration is enabled by default in this PR.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2C Discord opt-in broker routing proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Discord DMs, channels, and threads can route through broker-owned semantics while native Discord remains the default path. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, prefix-ownership contract, and truthful declared-capability boundary. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, explicit listed-provider gating, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram DMs and topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, inbound alias canonicalization, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds closed constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers without making BlueBubbles a separate platform identity. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2C - Discord opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. - Head: `370402e44f` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `370402e44f` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2C Discord broker routing proof on top of #86154, #86153, and #86096. - Normalizes broker-routed Discord DMs and channel threads without importing native Discord extension internals. - Preserves non-channel conversation type in canonical broker routes so Discord DMs remain direct routes. ## Rollout Context Closes #86111 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86154 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86139, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Discord defaults stay unchanged. Existing prefix ownership still keeps `discord:` owned by the native Discord plugin when installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:discord:...` and the broker alias path when native Discord is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Discord DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and provider-owned ids. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:discord:user:123456789012345678"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "discord-dm", "messageId": "native-discord-dm", "platform": "discord", "conversation": { "id": "123456789012345678", "type": "direct" }, "requirements": { "text": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced a direct Discord broker provider request rather than an ambiguous channel-looking target. - **What was not tested:** Live Discord API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can represent Discord DMs and channel threads through `channel-broker` using stable broker semantics: provider platform `discord`, direct/channel conversation type, conversation id, and optional thread id. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-discord-routing` - Head: `370402e44f` (current Discord proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:discord:user:123456789012345678`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "discord"`, `conversation.type: "direct"`, and `conversation.id: "123456789012345678"`. 3. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:discord:channel:222222222222222222?threadId=333333333333333333`. 4. Verified the provider request used channel conversation id plus `threadId`. 5. Resolved the outbound session route for the Discord DM target. 6. Verified the route stayed direct and canonicalized to `discord:direct%3A123456789012345678`. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 13 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Discord DMs and threads now flow through broker target parsing and provider request semantics, not through core or native Discord extension branches. ### Not Tested - Live Discord API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp

GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2A - Conformance and signed inbound baseline - Role: Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff with explicit listed-provider gating. - Head: `f6bf429242` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `f6bf429242` This branch also carries the tiny `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` timeout hardening inherited from #86096 after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally. Focused local validation after the latest package/deadcode/doc/capability repair and upstream/main rebase ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/http-routes.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec vitest run src/plugins/contracts/plugin-sdk-package-contract-guardrails.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.contracts-plugin.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.tooling.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts --maxWorkers=1` - `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts --maxWorkers=1` Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2A Channel Broker conformance baseline on top of #86096. - Extends `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` with inbound normalization, dedupe-key, provider capability normalization, and platform capability checks. - Adds bundled broker conformance coverage for durable inbound receive, durable final send receipts, retryable receipt failures, preview/progress capability gates, and `/verbose`/tool-message `channelData`. ## Rollout Context Closes #86109 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86133, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. This PR intentionally does not migrate Telegram, Discord, or Slack routing. Native defaults stay unchanged until the Phase 2B/2C/2D proofs land behind opt-in broker routing. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** The broker conformance baseline normalizes inbound events, builds durable dedupe keys, evaluates preview/progress capabilities, and sends outbound requests through the bundled broker lifecycle without platform-specific core branches. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that imported `src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts`, created a Telegram inbound event, built the durable dedupe key, evaluated Discord preview/progress capabilities, and sent broker outbound requests through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "inboundDedupeKey": "acme:bot-main:telegram:live-proof-telegram-1", "previewProgressSupported": true } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path produced the expected durable inbound key and capability decision outside the Vitest harness, proving the Phase 2A contract surface can carry the normalized lifecycle facts that later channel migrations consume. - **What was not tested:** Live provider network traffic and the full local suite; those remain covered by later provider proofs and GitHub Actions. ### Behavior The broker contract now has a conformance harness for the lifecycle paths that regress most often across maintained chat channels: inbound normalization/dedupe, durable final sends, provider receipts, retry/failure propagation, preview/progress capability checks, and `/verbose` tool-message metadata. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-conformance` - Head: `f6bf429242` (current Phase 2A head) ### Steps 1. Normalized a synthetic Telegram inbound event through the SDK and accepted it through the durable inbound receive journal. 2. Replayed the same dedupe key while pending and after completion to prove duplicate/redelivery handling. 3. Sent a synthetic Slack final message through the bundled broker runtime and verified provider receipt commit material. 4. Returned a retryable Discord provider receipt and verified the send fails with structured receipt context. 5. Built preview/finalize broker requests and asserted Discord preview/progress support through declared capabilities. 6. Passed `/verbose` tool-message policy through broker `channelData` without platform-specific branching. ### Evidence - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - 1 file passed, 7 tests passed. - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 3 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result The SDK helpers normalize provider payloads without dropping native metadata, the bundled plugin refuses non-sent provider receipts as durable-send failures, and conformance tests prove the current broker path can represent the Phase 2 lifecycle requirements without changing user-visible channel defaults. ### Not Tested - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. - Live Telegram, Discord, or Slack provider traffic. Those are intentionally scoped to Phase 2B/2C/2D. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.
